title: What are dunder methods
instruction:
Dunder methods (double underscore) are Python's special hooks. When you write `print(obj)`, Python calls `obj.__str__()`. When you write `len(obj)`, Python calls `obj.__len__()`.

They let your objects behave like Python built-ins.

Run this. Then add `__str__` to make print(book) show the title and author.
starter:
class Book:
    def __init__(self, title, author, pages):
        self.title = title
        self.author = author
        self.pages = pages

book = Book("Python Crash Course", "Eric Matthes", 544)
print(book)  # right now: <__main__.Book object at ...>
successMessage: Right now it shows memory address gibberish. Add __str__ and Python will call it whenever it needs to display your object as text.

title: __str__ vs __repr__
instruction:
`__str__` — human-readable output, used by `print()` and `str()`
`__repr__` — developer-friendly output, used in the Python shell, `repr()`, and debugging

Convention: `__repr__` should return something you could paste back into Python to recreate the object.

Add both to the `Book` class.
starter:
class Book:
    def __init__(self, title, author, pages):
        self.title = title
        self.author = author
        self.pages = pages
    
    # TODO: add __str__ and __repr__

book = Book("Python Crash Course", "Eric Matthes", 544)
print(book)    # uses __str__
print(repr(book))  # uses __repr__
expectedOutput: Python Crash Course by Eric Matthes

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: __str__ for humans, __repr__ for developers. Always implement both. If you only implement one, implement __repr__ — Python falls back to it for __str__ if needed.

title: __len__ — make len() work
instruction:
`len(obj)` calls `obj.__len__()`.

Add `__len__` to the `Playlist` class so `len(playlist)` returns the number of songs.
starter:
class Playlist:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        self.songs = []
    
    def add_song(self, song):
        self.songs.append(song)
    
    # TODO: add __len__ so len(playlist) works

playlist = Playlist("Favourites")
playlist.add_song("Song A")
playlist.add_song("Song B")
playlist.add_song("Song C")
print(len(playlist))  # 3

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: len(playlist) now works just like len([1,2,3]). Your class is starting to feel like a built-in Python type.

title: __eq__ — make == work
instruction:
By default, `==` on objects checks if they are the **same object** (same memory address). 

To compare by **value**, implement `__eq__`:

```python
def __eq__(self, other):
    return self.value == other.value
```

Add `__eq__` to `Point` so two points with same x and y are considered equal.
hint:
def __eq__(self, other):
    return self.x == other.x and self.y == other.y
starter:
class Point:
    def __init__(self, x, y):
        self.x = x
        self.y = y
    
    # TODO: add __eq__ to compare by x and y values

p1 = Point(3, 4)
p2 = Point(3, 4)
p3 = Point(1, 2)

print(p1 == p2)  # should be True
print(p1 == p3)  # should be False

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Two different objects, same values, equal. Without __eq__ they would never be equal — they are different objects in memory. Value equality vs identity.

title: __lt__ — make sorting work
instruction:
To sort a list of custom objects with `sorted()`, you need `__lt__` (less than).

Add `__lt__` to `Student` so you can sort by score.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: sorted() now works because Python can compare students by score. Add __gt__, __le__, __ge__ for full comparison support — or use @functools.total_ordering.

title: __add__ — operator overloading
instruction:
`__add__` makes the `+` operator work on your class:

`v1 + v2` calls `v1.__add__(v2)`

Add `__add__` to `Vector` so you can add two vectors together.
hint:
def __add__(self, other):
    return Vector(self.x + other.x, self.y + other.y)

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: v1 + v2 creates a new Vector(4, 6). Operator overloading makes your class feel native. You can also add __sub__, __mul__, __truediv__ for full math support.

title: __contains__ — make 'in' work
instruction:
`x in obj` calls `obj.__contains__(x)`.

Add `__contains__` to `Playlist` so you can use `"Song A" in playlist`.
hint:
def __contains__(self, song):
    return song in self.songs
starter:
class Playlist:
    def __init__(self):
        self.songs = []
    
    def add(self, song):
        self.songs.append(song)
    
    # TODO: add __contains__ so 'song in playlist' works

playlist = Playlist()
playlist.add("Bohemian Rhapsody")
playlist.add("Hotel California")

print("Hotel California" in playlist)  # True
print("Stairway to Heaven" in playlist)  # False

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: in now works on your Playlist. __contains__ is what makes custom collections feel like Python built-ins.

title: Build a money class
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a `Money` class with:
- `__init__(amount, currency)` — store amount and currency
- `__str__` — returns e.g. `"$10.00"`
- `__repr__` — returns e.g. `"Money(10.0, 'USD')"`
- `__add__` — add two Money objects (same currency only)
- `__eq__` — equal if same amount and currency

Test all operations.
successMessage: A Money class that behaves like a first-class Python type. You can print it, add it, compare it. Dunder methods transform plain classes into powerful objects. Chapter 16 done.
